summary: DHH describes the final stages of preparing the first ONCE product for launch, emphasizing their commitment to exceptionally high code quality. Because ONCE ships its source code with purchase, the team has held themselves to watchmaker-level standards of internal polish. He argues that building for quality and readability actually accelerates delivery rather than slowing it down, countering the conventional wisdom that crunch and corner-cutting are necessary to meet deadlines. He personally reviews all 7,412 lines of code repeatedly to refine naming, spacing, and cohesion.
key_points: Shipping source code with the product creates accountability for internal code quality, preventing the temptation to cut corners
High internal quality standards serve commercial deadlines rather than opposing them — 'slow is smooth, smooth is fast'
Repeated full codebase reviews catch not just style issues but also functional quality problems that reduce customer issues
The ONCE business model (buy once, get the code) enables a new relationship with customers through code walkthroughs
money_quotes: We've handcuffed ourselves to the virtue of beautiful code by committing to shipping the code with purchase.
I want to show everyone that an extremely high level of care for the internals is not in opposition to the demands of commercial deadlines.
In racing, like other communities of competence, you'll often hear the saying: Slow is smooth. Smooth is fast.

Building for quality, building for readability, and sweating the details is a slow, smooth process. One that gets you to release quicker than the frantic thrashing you hear about from stereotypical crunch sessions.
We want ONCE #1 to have Patek levels of finishing, because we're shipping this product with an exhibition case back.
Every time I do, I usually also find other small quality issues that'll lead to a better product which fewer people will have issues with.
key_insight: Shipping your source code to customers is a powerful forcing function for maintaining high internal quality, and that discipline pays off in faster, smoother delivery.
